meta.notAffiliated: Independent practice tool. Not affiliated with, endorsed by, or connected to the Home Office or UK Government.

_fieldGuide.id: Unique, stable ID. Never reuse a retired ID.
_fieldGuide.category: One of: values | uk-overview | history | society | government
_fieldGuide.tier: 1-4 difficulty (1 = easiest, 4 = hardest) for the climb mechanic.
_fieldGuide.question: Your OWN wording. Never copy a handbook sentence.
_fieldGuide.answer: single: index of correct option. multi: array of correct indices.
_fieldGuide.explanation: Short, original explanation shown after answering.
_fieldGuide.fact: Internal note of the underlying fact (not shown to users).
_fieldGuide.volatile: true if the answer is a figure/name that can change. Re-check each cycle.
_fieldGuide.lastReviewed: ISO date this item was last fact-checked.

questions.question: In which year did the Battle of Hastings take place?
questions.explanation: William of Normandy defeated King Harold at the Battle of Hastings in 1066, beginning Norman rule in England.

questions.question: Which document, agreed in 1215, limited the power of the monarch?
questions.explanation: Magna Carta, agreed by King John in 1215, established that the king was subject to the law.

questions.question: During whose reign was the Spanish Armada defeated in 1588?
questions.explanation: The Spanish Armada was defeated in 1588 during the reign of Elizabeth I.

questions.question: Who commanded the British fleet to victory at the Battle of Trafalgar in 1805?
questions.explanation: Admiral Nelson led the British navy to victory at Trafalgar in 1805 and was killed in the battle; Nelson's Column commemorates him.

questions.question: Who led the forces that defeated Napoleon at Waterloo in 1815?
questions.explanation: The Duke of Wellington commanded the allied forces that defeated Napoleon at Waterloo in 1815.

questions.question: In which year did the Great Fire of London occur?
questions.explanation: The Great Fire of London took place in 1666, destroying much of the city.

questions.question: The Gunpowder Plot of 1605 was a failed attempt to do what?
options: Blow up the Houses of Parliament
questions.explanation: In 1605 a group including Guy Fawkes tried to blow up Parliament. It is remembered each year on Bonfire Night, 5 November.

questions.question: The Act of Union of 1707 joined England with which nation to form Great Britain?
questions.explanation: The Act of Union of 1707 united England (and Wales) with Scotland to create the Kingdom of Great Britain.

questions.question: In which year did women over the age of 30 first gain the right to vote in the UK?
questions.explanation: Women over 30 won the vote in 1918. Voting was equalised with men, at age 21, in 1928.

questions.question: In which year was the National Health Service (NHS) established?
questions.explanation: The NHS was founded in 1948, providing healthcare free at the point of use.

questions.question: The Domesday Book, a survey of England, was ordered by which king?
questions.explanation: William the Conqueror ordered the Domesday Book in 1086 to record landholdings across England.

questions.question: Henry VIII broke away from the Church of Rome and established which church?
questions.explanation: Henry VIII's break with Rome led to the founding of the Church of England, with the monarch as its head.
questions.fact: Henry VIII founded the Church of England.

questions.question: Which wall did the Romans build to defend against tribes from the north?
questions.explanation: Hadrian's Wall was built on the orders of the Emperor Hadrian to keep out tribes from the north of Britain.
questions.fact: Hadrian's Wall, built under Emperor Hadrian.

questions.question: What was the name of the plague that killed much of England's population in the Middle Ages?
questions.explanation: The Black Death reached England in 1348 and killed a large proportion of the population.

questions.question: The Wars of the Roses were fought between the House of Lancaster and which other house?
questions.explanation: The Wars of the Roses (1455-1485) were a civil war between the Houses of Lancaster and York. They ended when Henry Tudor became Henry VII.
questions.fact: Wars of the Roses: Lancaster vs York.

questions.question: How many wives did King Henry VIII have?
questions.explanation: Henry VIII had six wives during his reign.

questions.question: Which king was executed in 1649 after the English Civil War?
questions.explanation: Charles I was executed in 1649 following defeat in the English Civil War, and England briefly became a republic.

questions.question: Who led the Parliamentary forces and became Lord Protector after the Civil War?
questions.explanation: Oliver Cromwell led the Parliamentary forces and ruled as Lord Protector during the period England was a republic.

questions.question: The overthrow of King James II in 1688 is known as what?
questions.explanation: The Glorious Revolution of 1688 saw James II replaced by William of Orange and Mary as joint monarchs.

questions.question: Britain is regarded as the first country to undergo which major transformation in the 18th and 19th centuries?
questions.explanation: Britain was the first country to industrialise, in what became known as the Industrial Revolution.

questions.question: Isambard Kingdom Brunel is famous for being a great British what?
questions.explanation: Brunel was a celebrated engineer who built railways, bridges, tunnels and ships.

questions.question: Florence Nightingale is best known for founding modern what?
questions.explanation: Florence Nightingale is regarded as the founder of modern nursing, having organised care for soldiers in the Crimean War.

questions.question: William Wilberforce is associated with the campaign to abolish what?
questions.explanation: William Wilberforce was a leading campaigner against the slave trade, which was abolished in the British Empire in 1807.
questions.fact: Wilberforce = abolition of the slave trade (1807).

questions.question: In which year did the First World War begin?
questions.explanation: The First World War began in 1914 and ended in 1918.

questions.question: Who was the British Prime Minister for most of the Second World War?
questions.explanation: Winston Churchill was Prime Minister for most of the Second World War and led Britain through it.

questions.question: The Battle of Britain in 1940 was fought mainly in which arena?
questions.explanation: The Battle of Britain in 1940 was an air campaign in which the RAF defended Britain against the German air force.

questions.question: Who is generally regarded as the first British Prime Minister?
questions.explanation: Sir Robert Walpole, from 1721, is generally regarded as the first British Prime Minister.

questions.question: The Great Exhibition of 1851 was held in which structure in London's Hyde Park?
questions.explanation: The Great Exhibition of 1851 was held in the Crystal Palace, a huge structure of glass and iron in Hyde Park.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT part of the United Kingdom?
questions.explanation: The UK is made up of England, Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland. The Republic of Ireland is an independent country.

questions.question: What is the capital city of Wales?
questions.explanation: Cardiff is the capital of Wales and home to the Senedd, the Welsh Parliament.

questions.question: What is the capital city of Scotland?
questions.explanation: Edinburgh is the capital of Scotland and the seat of the Scottish Parliament.

questions.question: What is the currency of the UK?
questions.explanation: The currency of the UK is the pound sterling (£), divided into 100 pence.

questions.question: What is the highest mountain in the UK?
questions.explanation: Ben Nevis, in the Scottish Highlands, is the highest mountain in the UK.

questions.question: What is the capital city of Northern Ireland?
questions.explanation: Belfast is the capital city of Northern Ireland.

questions.question: What is the capital city of the UK?
questions.explanation: London is the capital city of the UK and of England.

questions.question: What is the longest river in the UK?
questions.explanation: The River Severn is the longest river in the UK, slightly longer than the Thames.

questions.question: Great Britain is made up of England, Scotland and which other country?
questions.explanation: Great Britain refers to England, Scotland and Wales. Adding Northern Ireland makes the United Kingdom.

questions.question: The official flag of the UK is commonly known as the:
questions.explanation: The UK flag is the Union Flag, often called the Union Jack, combining the crosses of the patron saints.
questions.fact: UK flag = Union Flag / Union Jack.

questions.question: Which body of water separates Great Britain from France?
questions.explanation: The English Channel separates the south of Great Britain from northern France.
questions.fact: GB and France separated by the English Channel.

questions.question: Who is the patron saint of Scotland?
questions.explanation: St Andrew is the patron saint of Scotland. (George - England, David - Wales, Patrick - Northern Ireland.)
questions.fact: Patron saint of Scotland = St Andrew.

questions.question: On what date is St David's Day celebrated?
questions.explanation: St David's Day, for the patron saint of Wales, is held on 1 March.

questions.question: Which flower is traditionally associated with England?
questions.explanation: The rose is the national flower of England. (Scotland - thistle, Wales - daffodil, Northern Ireland - shamrock.)

questions.question: Which Scottish poet wrote the words to 'Auld Lang Syne'?
questions.explanation: Robert Burns, Scotland's national poet, wrote 'Auld Lang Syne', traditionally sung at New Year.

questions.question: In which town was William Shakespeare born?
questions.explanation: Shakespeare was born in Stratford-upon-Avon, where his birthplace is still visited today.

questions.question: Which TWO of the following are British Overseas Territories?
questions.explanation: Gibraltar and the Falkland Islands are British Overseas Territories. The Republic of Ireland is independent and France is unconnected to the UK.
questions.fact: Overseas Territories include Gibraltar and the Falklands.

questions.question: Which flower is associated with Scotland?
questions.explanation: The thistle is the national flower of Scotland.

questions.question: Which flower is associated with Wales?
questions.explanation: The daffodil is associated with Wales (the leek is also a Welsh emblem).

questions.question: On what date is St George's Day, England's national day, celebrated?
questions.explanation: St George's Day is celebrated on 23 April.

questions.question: On what date is Bonfire Night held?
questions.explanation: Bonfire Night is held on 5 November, marking the failure of the Gunpowder Plot in 1605.

questions.question: On what date is Remembrance Day held each year?
questions.explanation: Remembrance Day is held on 11 November to honour those who died in wars; many people wear poppies.

questions.question: Hogmanay is the Scottish celebration of what?
questions.explanation: Hogmanay is the Scottish name for New Year's Eve celebrations.

questions.question: Who is the patron saint of Northern Ireland?
questions.explanation: St Patrick is the patron saint of Northern Ireland, celebrated on 17 March.
questions.fact: Patron saint of NI = St Patrick.

questions.question: Wimbledon is a world-famous British event in which sport?
questions.explanation: Wimbledon, held in London, is the oldest tennis tournament in the world.

questions.question: The Grand National is a famous British example of which sport?
questions.explanation: The Grand National is a celebrated annual horse race held at Aintree.

questions.question: Which prehistoric monument is found in Wiltshire, England?
questions.explanation: Stonehenge, in Wiltshire, is one of the most famous prehistoric monuments in the world.

questions.question: What traditional dish is eaten at a Burns Night supper?
questions.explanation: Haggis is traditionally served at a Burns Night supper, held around 25 January to celebrate Robert Burns.

questions.question: How many pence are there in one pound sterling?
questions.explanation: There are 100 pence in one pound sterling.

questions.question: The UK is best described as a:
questions.explanation: The UK is a constitutional monarchy: the monarch is head of state, but elected representatives in Parliament make the laws.

questions.question: How many members does the Northern Ireland Assembly have?
questions.explanation: The Northern Ireland Assembly has 90 members (MLAs), five for each of the 18 constituencies. It was reduced from 108 in 2017.

questions.question: What is the official London home of the Prime Minister?
questions.explanation: The Prime Minister's official residence is 10 Downing Street.

questions.question: At least how often must a UK general election be held?
questions.explanation: A general election must be held at least every five years.
questions.fact: General election at least every 5 years.

questions.question: The two Houses of the UK Parliament are the House of Commons and the:
questions.explanation: Parliament is made up of the House of Commons and the House of Lords, together with the monarch.

questions.question: People elected to the House of Commons are known as:
questions.explanation: People elected to represent constituencies in the House of Commons are called Members of Parliament, or MPs.

questions.question: Which voting system is used to elect MPs in UK general elections?
questions.explanation: MPs are elected using the 'first past the post' system, where the candidate with the most votes in a constituency wins.
questions.fact: General elections use first past the post.

questions.question: Who chairs debates in the House of Commons?
questions.explanation: The Speaker keeps order and chairs debates in the House of Commons, and is politically neutral.
questions.fact: Commons debates chaired by the Speaker.

questions.question: Civil servants in the UK are expected to be politically:
questions.explanation: Civil servants are politically neutral and must serve whichever government is in power.

questions.question: Where does the Scottish Parliament sit?
questions.explanation: The Scottish Parliament sits at Holyrood in Edinburgh.

questions.question: What is the name of the Welsh Parliament?
questions.explanation: The Welsh Parliament is called the Senedd, based in Cardiff.

questions.question: Who is the head of state of the UK?
questions.explanation: The monarch (the King or Queen) is the head of state, while the Prime Minister is the head of government.

questions.question: What is the highest court of appeal in the UK?
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court, created in 2009, is the highest court of appeal in the UK.
questions.fact: Highest court of appeal = the Supreme Court.

questions.question: Who leads the Cabinet of senior ministers?
questions.explanation: The Prime Minister leads the Cabinet, the group of senior ministers responsible for running government departments.
questions.fact: Cabinet led by the Prime Minister.

questions.question: Which of the following is a fundamental principle of British life?
questions.explanation: The rule of law is one of the fundamental principles of British life, alongside democracy, individual liberty and tolerance.
questions.fact: Rule of law is a fundamental principle.

questions.question: Which of these is described as a responsibility of people living in the UK?
options: Treating others with fairness and respect
Attending a place of worship weekly
questions.explanation: Treating others with fairness and respect, and obeying the law, are responsibilities shared by everyone in the UK.
questions.fact: Responsibility = treat others fairly, obey the law.

questions.question: Which of these is one of the fundamental principles of British life?
options: Tolerance of those with different faiths and beliefs
questions.explanation: Tolerance of those with different faiths and beliefs is a fundamental principle, alongside democracy, the rule of law and individual liberty.
questions.fact: Tolerance of different faiths is a fundamental principle.

questions.question: Participation in which of the following is encouraged as part of being a good citizen?
questions.explanation: Participating in the life of your community is part of being a good citizen and resident in the UK.
questions.fact: Participation in community life is encouraged.

questions.question: In around which year did the Roman army leave Britain?
questions.explanation: The Romans left Britain in around AD 410, after nearly 400 years of rule.

questions.question: The Vikings who raided Britain came from which part of the world?
questions.explanation: The Vikings came from Scandinavia — present-day Denmark and Norway — first raiding Britain in the 8th and 9th centuries.

questions.question: The Hundred Years War was fought between England and which country?
questions.explanation: The Hundred Years War was a long conflict between England and France during the Middle Ages.
questions.fact: Hundred Years War: England vs France.

questions.question: Sir Isaac Newton is remembered as a famous British what?
questions.explanation: Sir Isaac Newton was a hugely influential scientist, known for his work on gravity and motion.

questions.question: In which year did the Second World War end?
questions.explanation: The Second World War ended in 1945.

questions.question: How many countries make up the United Kingdom?
questions.explanation: The UK is made up of four countries: England, Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland.

questions.question: Which of these is a Crown Dependency, and not part of the UK?
questions.explanation: The Isle of Man and the Channel Islands are Crown Dependencies — linked to the UK but not part of it.
questions.fact: Isle of Man and Channel Islands are Crown Dependencies, not part of the UK.

questions.question: The flag of Scotland shows the cross of which patron saint?
questions.explanation: The Scottish flag, the Saltire, shows the white diagonal cross of St Andrew on a blue background.
questions.fact: Scottish flag = St Andrew's cross (the Saltire).

questions.question: 'Big Ben' is the nickname of the great bell at which building?
questions.explanation: 'Big Ben' is the nickname for the great bell in the clock tower at the Houses of Parliament in London.
questions.fact: Big Ben = great bell at the Houses of Parliament.

questions.question: What is the highest mountain in Wales?
questions.explanation: Snowdon (Yr Wyddfa) is the highest mountain in Wales. Ben Nevis is the highest in the whole UK.

questions.question: On what date is St Patrick's Day celebrated?
questions.explanation: St Patrick's Day, for the patron saint of Northern Ireland, is celebrated on 17 March.

questions.question: Which flower do people traditionally wear in November to remember those who died in wars?
questions.explanation: People wear a poppy in the run-up to Remembrance Day on 11 November.

questions.question: On what date is Christmas Day celebrated in the UK?
questions.explanation: Christmas Day is celebrated on 25 December. 26 December is Boxing Day.

questions.question: Diwali, the festival of lights, is mainly celebrated by which communities?
questions.explanation: Diwali, the festival of lights, is celebrated mainly by Hindus and Sikhs.
questions.fact: Diwali is celebrated by Hindus and Sikhs.

questions.question: Which UK city hosts a world-famous arts festival each summer, including the Fringe?
questions.explanation: Edinburgh hosts a major arts festival each year, including the famous Edinburgh Festival Fringe.
questions.fact: Edinburgh hosts the Festival and Fringe.

questions.question: Charles Dickens was a famous British what?
questions.explanation: Charles Dickens was a celebrated novelist whose works include Oliver Twist and Great Expectations.

questions.question: 'The Proms' is an annual series of which kind of concert?
questions.explanation: The Proms is a series of classical music concerts held each summer, ending with the Last Night of the Proms.

questions.question: How do most members of the House of Lords come to be there?
questions.explanation: Most members of the House of Lords are appointed; they are not elected by the public.
questions.fact: House of Lords members are appointed, not elected.

questions.question: In political matters, the monarch is expected to remain:
questions.explanation: The monarch is politically neutral and acts on the advice of the government.

questions.question: Which of these is a responsibility that has been devolved to Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland?
questions.explanation: Matters such as education and health are devolved, while defence, foreign policy and immigration remain with the UK government.
questions.fact: Education is devolved; defence, foreign policy and immigration are not.

questions.question: The UK's constitution is unusual because it is:
options: Not written down in any one place
questions.explanation: The UK does not have a single written constitution; it is 'unwritten', built up over time from laws, conventions and documents.
questions.fact: The UK has an uncodified (unwritten) constitution.

questions.question: Local services run by councils are paid for partly through which tax?
questions.explanation: Council Tax helps pay for local services such as rubbish collection and local roads.
questions.fact: Local council services are funded partly by Council Tax.

questions.question: After a general election, who normally becomes Prime Minister?
options: The leader of the party with the most MPs
questions.explanation: The leader of the party that wins the most seats in the House of Commons normally becomes Prime Minister.
questions.fact: PM = leader of the party with the most MPs.

questions.question: Which of these is a fundamental principle of British life?
questions.explanation: Democracy is one of the fundamental principles of British life.

questions.question: Which of these is one of the fundamental principles of British life?
options: A single official language required by law
questions.explanation: Individual liberty is a fundamental principle, alongside democracy, the rule of law, tolerance and participation.
questions.fact: Individual liberty is a fundamental principle.

questions.question: Which of these is a freedom that people in the UK enjoy?
options: Freedom to drive without a licence
questions.explanation: Freedom of speech is one of the freedoms protected in the UK, within the limits of the law.
questions.fact: Freedom of speech is a protected freedom.

questions.question: Which of these is a freedom protected in the UK?
questions.explanation: Freedom of belief and religion is one of the freedoms people in the UK enjoy.
questions.fact: Freedom of belief and religion is protected.

questions.question: As part of respecting shared values, residents are expected to look after what?
options: The area they live in and the environment
questions.explanation: Looking after the area you live in and the environment is one of the responsibilities of living in the UK.
questions.fact: Responsibility: look after your area and the environment.

questions.question: At a citizenship ceremony, new citizens take an oath or affirmation of:
questions.explanation: At a citizenship ceremony, new citizens take an oath or affirmation of allegiance and make a pledge to the UK.
questions.fact: Citizenship ceremony = oath/affirmation of allegiance plus a pledge.

questions.question: Britain's earliest human inhabitants lived during which age?
questions.explanation: Britain's first people were Stone Age hunter-gatherers.

questions.question: After the Romans left, Britain was settled by the Anglo-Saxons, who came from where?
options: Northern Europe, including Germany and Denmark
questions.explanation: The Anglo-Saxons came from what is now northern Germany, Denmark and the Netherlands.

questions.question: Which religion spread across Britain in the Anglo-Saxon period, helped by missionaries such as St Augustine?
questions.explanation: Christianity spread through Britain during the Anglo-Saxon period.

questions.question: In 1603, James VI of Scotland also became king of England. By what name was he known as England's king?
questions.explanation: James VI of Scotland became James I of England in 1603, uniting the crowns.
questions.fact: James VI of Scotland = James I of England (1603).

questions.question: Which architect designed the new St Paul's Cathedral after the Great Fire of London?
questions.explanation: Sir Christopher Wren designed the new St Paul's Cathedral, rebuilt after the Great Fire of 1666.
questions.fact: St Paul's rebuilt by Sir Christopher Wren.

questions.question: Mary, Queen of Scots was eventually executed on the orders of which English queen?
questions.explanation: Mary, Queen of Scots was executed during the reign of Elizabeth I.
questions.fact: Mary, Queen of Scots executed under Elizabeth I.

questions.question: The Act of Union of 1801 joined Great Britain with which country?
questions.explanation: The Act of Union of 1801 created the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland.
questions.fact: Act of Union 1801 = Great Britain + Ireland.

questions.question: Who became the UK's first woman Prime Minister, in 1979?
questions.explanation: Margaret Thatcher became the UK's first woman Prime Minister in 1979.

questions.question: In which year did the UK switch to decimal currency?
questions.explanation: The UK adopted decimal currency in 1971.

questions.question: Sir Frank Whittle is credited with developing which invention?
questions.explanation: Sir Frank Whittle, a British engineer, developed the jet engine.

questions.question: Which British inventor created the World Wide Web?
questions.explanation: Sir Tim Berners-Lee, a British scientist, invented the World Wide Web.
questions.fact: World Wide Web = Sir Tim Berners-Lee.

questions.question: Alexander Fleming is famous for discovering which medicine?
questions.explanation: Alexander Fleming discovered penicillin, the first widely used antibiotic.

questions.question: What appears on the national flag of Wales?
questions.explanation: The Welsh flag features a red dragon on a green and white background.

questions.question: The Channel Tunnel connects England with which country?
questions.explanation: The Channel Tunnel links England and France under the sea.
questions.fact: Channel Tunnel connects England and France.

questions.question: Which river flows through London?
questions.explanation: The River Thames flows through London.

questions.question: The Giant's Causeway is a famous natural landmark in which part of the UK?
questions.explanation: The Giant's Causeway, a landscape of basalt columns, is in Northern Ireland.
questions.fact: Giant's Causeway is in Northern Ireland.

questions.question: The Union Flag does not include a symbol representing which part of the UK?
questions.explanation: Wales is not represented on the Union Flag, as it was already united with England when the flag was first created.
questions.fact: Union Flag does not represent Wales.

questions.question: Which is the most popular spectator sport in the UK?
questions.explanation: Football is the most popular sport in the UK.

questions.question: The Six Nations championship is contested in which sport?
questions.explanation: The Six Nations is an annual rugby union competition between six European nations.

questions.question: The Scottish town of St Andrews is known as the home of which sport?
questions.explanation: St Andrews in Scotland is regarded as the home of golf.

questions.question: The Beatles, the famous 1960s band, came from which city?
questions.explanation: The Beatles came from Liverpool.

questions.question: Which of these was a famous British painter?
questions.explanation: John Constable was a celebrated British landscape painter. (Dickens was a novelist, Newton a scientist, Wren an architect.)

questions.question: Jane Austen is famous as a British what?
questions.explanation: Jane Austen was a novelist, known for works such as Pride and Prejudice.

questions.question: What is the minimum age to buy alcohol in a pub or shop in the UK?
questions.explanation: You must normally be at least 18 to buy alcohol in the UK.

questions.question: On what date is Halloween celebrated?
questions.explanation: Halloween is celebrated on 31 October.

questions.question: On what date is Valentine's Day?
questions.explanation: Valentine's Day is celebrated on 14 February.

questions.question: Which plant is the traditional emblem of Northern Ireland?
questions.explanation: The shamrock is the traditional emblem of Northern Ireland.

questions.question: How many people make up a jury in a Crown Court in England and Wales?
questions.explanation: A jury in a Crown Court in England and Wales has 12 members. (In Scotland it is 15.)

questions.question: The UK is a member of which international defence alliance?
questions.explanation: The UK is a member of NATO, the North Atlantic Treaty Organisation.

questions.question: Who is the head of the Commonwealth?
questions.explanation: The monarch is the head of the Commonwealth, a group of countries that mostly have historic links to Britain.
questions.fact: Head of the Commonwealth = the monarch.

questions.question: The second-largest party in the House of Commons forms what?
questions.explanation: The largest party not in government forms the official Opposition, which challenges and scrutinises the government.

questions.question: The police in the UK are expected to be:
options: Honest, professional and free from political bias
questions.explanation: The police must be honest and professional, and act impartially, free from political interference.
questions.fact: Police are impartial and free from political bias.

questions.question: Where can people meet their local MP to raise concerns?
questions.explanation: MPs hold regular 'surgeries' where constituents can come to discuss their concerns.

questions.question: Elections to the Scottish Parliament and the Welsh Senedd use which kind of voting system?
questions.explanation: The devolved parliaments are elected using forms of proportional representation, rather than the first-past-the-post system used for Westminster.

questions.question: Judges in the UK are expected to be independent of whom?
questions.explanation: The judiciary is independent of government, ensuring the law is applied fairly.

questions.question: Which of these is a right that people in the UK have?
options: The right to a fair trial
The right to break the law
The right to a free car
questions.explanation: The right to a fair trial is one of the rights protected in the UK.
questions.fact: Right to a fair trial is protected.

questions.question: Serving on a jury when called is an example of a citizen's:
questions.explanation: Serving on a jury when called is one of the responsibilities of living in the UK.

questions.question: Which of these is a responsibility of people living in the UK?
questions.explanation: Paying taxes is one of the responsibilities of living in the UK.

questions.question: Which of these is a positive way to take part in your community?
questions.explanation: Volunteering and helping others is encouraged as part of being a good citizen.

questions.question: Which of these is a freedom protected in the UK?
questions.explanation: Freedom from unfair discrimination is one of the freedoms protected in the UK.
questions.fact: Freedom from unfair discrimination is protected.
